profile_summary: 작고 빠른 하드웨어에 맞춘 메모리·GPU 시스템을 설계하여 제품 수준의 ML을 구현합니다.
sidebar_profile_body: 온디바이스 AI와 GPU 메모리 시스템 연구를 수행하는 연구원 이준헌입니다. 제한된 하드웨어 환경에서도 대규모 모델을 안정적으로 실행할 수 있는 구조적 최적화 방안을 탐구합니다.
intro_body: 페이지 상단 크레딧을 따라가며 각 카드를 클릭하면 프로젝트/논문 상세를 볼 수 있습니다.
home_intro_desc: 엔지니어링 결과와 연구를 필터로 구분하고 각 상세를 확인해보세요.
portfolio_intro_desc: 참여하고 수행한 연구와 프로젝트를 정리한 페이지입니다.
portfolio_projects_desc: 각 카드가 프로젝트 상세 페이지로 연결됩니다.
resume_intro_desc: 핸즈온 경험, 논문, 수상 기록을 정리했습니다.

detail_esmoe_overview: 희소 전문가 라우팅과 관측 스택으로 저지연 추론을 유지합니다.

detail_cxl_point1: 텐서를 CXL로 옮길지 결정하는 지연 예측.
detail_5g_subtitle: Bringing Open5GS, srsRAN, and O-RAN SC together
detail_5g_overview: Recreated an end-to-end 5G stack connecting Open5GS (Core), srsRAN gNB/UE, and the O-RAN SC RIC over E2, while preserving every bring-up artifact (configs, Docker networking, namespaces) so the lab report is reproducible.
detail_5g_point1: Scripted the srsRAN combo (`docker compose up 5gc`, `./gnb -c gnb_zmq.yaml`, `./srsue ue_zmq.conf`) with explicit namespaces, ports, and NAT tweaks to prove attachment from Core to UE.
detail_5g_point2: Started the O-RAN SC RIC and tied it to the gNB to validate the E2 control-plane handshake as soon as both containers were available.
detail_5g_point3: Tracked gNB startup failures by auditing AMF reachability, YAML/PCAP settings, and permissions; when the INI parser stalled, we refreshed the build and rerouted to a known-good workflow.
detail_5g_outcome: Captured the commands, configs, and debugging notes so future teams can reproduce the Core→RIC→UE cycle without hunting for missing files.
detail_5g_arch_body: UE (srsRAN) ↔ gNB (srsRAN) ↔ Open5GS Core with the O-RAN SC RIC attached via 5G RAN E2 interfaces; every service runs in Docker Compose, networks isolated with namespaces, NAT rules, and service labels.
detail_5g_pipeline_step1: Start Open5GS 5gc container (`docker compose up 5gc`), confirm AMF/SMF/NSSF services and northbound gNodeB/IP reachability.
detail_5g_pipeline_step2: Bring up the O-RAN SC RIC so E2AP stacks can register gobally; inspect `ric` logs for heartbeat then set RAN node config to open E2 connections.
detail_5g_pipeline_step3: Launch srsRAN gNB with E2 enabled (`./gnb -c gnb_zmq.yaml e2`) in the same namespace, verifying gNB local interfaces plus correct PCAP path/version.
detail_5g_pipeline_step4: Activate srsRAN UE (`./srsue ue_zmq.conf`), observe NAS attach, then run `ping`/`traceroute` through the namespace to prove UE ↔ Core connectivity.
detail_5g_validation_item1: Open5GS health check shows AMF/NSSF threads bound to 38412 and publishes service stats before gNB startup.
detail_5g_validation_item2: RIC reports E2 session establishment when the gNB registers, so the control plane handshake was verified via the REST debug API.
detail_5g_validation_item3: UE attaches and receives IP; `ping`/`iperf` from the UE namespace to the core uplink succeed, confirming N2/N3 path.
detail_5g_debug_symptom: srsRAN gNodeB would exit before establishing an E2 session; logs pointed to E2AP initialization failures.
detail_5g_debug_hypothesis: AMF address mismatch, missing `gnb_e2ap.pcap`, or config drift caused by outdated repo versions.
detail_5g_debug_tests: Checked AMF reachability via `docker exec open5gs_5gc ping`, verified E2AP PCAP file exists with correct permissions, compared gnb_zmq.yaml versus the known-good template.
detail_5g_debug_fix: Rebuilt gNB container with the latest upstream repo, re-generated configs, and re-synchronized the PCAP path/permissions so E2AP parser sees a valid file.
detail_5g_debug_takeaway: Documented the workflow, pinned repo versions, and added scripts so future attempts start from this known-good state.
detail_5g_skills_item1: Container orchestration and container-networking design (Docker Compose, namespaces, NAT) for PR-ready demos.
detail_5g_skills_item2: RIC/E2 integration and REST debug tooling to prove control plane readiness before exposing the stack to RAN nodes.
detail_5g_skills_item3: Systematic debugging across AMF, E2AP, and PCAP layers, documenting hypotheses/tests/fixes for the team.
detail_5g_skills_item4: Reproducible engineering documentation that doubles as a hiring artifact.
work_opencl_title: On-Device GPU Image Processing via OpenCL
detail_opencl_title: On-Device GPU Image Processing via OpenCL & Android NDK
detail_opencl_subtitle: 8.4× Gaussian blur speedup through JNI-bridged GPGPU kernels on Android
detail_opencl_overview: Built an Android application that captures images via the device camera and applies real-time image processing—Gaussian blur, grayscale conversion, and rotation—by dispatching OpenCL kernels to the mobile GPU through a JNI-bridged native library. The project was the final deliverable for a microprocessor systems lab course and served as a hands-on introduction to GPGPU programming on a resource-constrained embedded platform where publicly available documentation was minimal.
detail_opencl_point1: Implemented a full GPGPU pipeline in C using OpenCL 1.2: platform/device enumeration, context and command-queue creation, kernel compilation at runtime, buffer I/O (clCreateBuffer / clEnqueueWriteBuffer / clEnqueueReadBuffer), and explicit resource release — entirely from scratch on Android NDK.
detail_opencl_point2: Achieved an 8.4× end-to-end speedup for Gaussian blur by offloading pixel-parallel computation to the GPU (CPU: 2059 ms → GPU: 246 ms), and additionally implemented Grayscale (81 ms) and Rotate (72 ms) kernels demonstrating sustained GPU throughput across heterogeneous workloads.
detail_opencl_point3: Designed a CMake + NDK build pipeline that links libGLES_mali.so and jnigraphics, compiles OpenCLDriver.c as a shared library, and exposes four JNI-callable native methods (GaussianBlurBitmap, GaussianBlurGPU, GaussianGreyGPU, GaussianRotateGPU) to the Java activity layer.
detail_opencl_arch_body: Android Java Activity (UI + Button events) calls JNI native methods in OpenCLDriver.c. The driver reads ARGB_8888 bitmap pixels via AndroidBitmap_lockPixels, copies raw pixel data into a staging buffer, then initializes the OpenCL runtime (clGetPlatformIDs → clGetDeviceIDs → clCreateContext → clCreateCommandQueue). It compiles the appropriate .cl kernel file at runtime (clCreateProgramWithSource + clBuildProgram), enqueues the NDRange kernel over all pixels, reads results back, releases all CL resources, and returns the processed Bitmap to Java for display. Three separate .cl files cover Blur, Grayscale, and Rotate operations, sharing the same driver flow with only the kernel name and file path differing.
detail_opencl_pipeline_step1: Set up Android project with CMake 3.22, configure AndroidManifest.xml for CAMERA permission, link OpenCL and jnigraphics libraries via target_link_libraries in CMakeLists.txt.
detail_opencl_pipeline_step2: Implement OpenCLDriver.c: read bitmap metadata (width, height, stride, RGBA format check), copy pixel data to a native staging buffer, and initialize the full OpenCL runtime context and command queue.
detail_opencl_pipeline_step3: Load and compile .cl kernel files at runtime (clCreateProgramWithSource → clBuildProgram), set kernel arguments for source buffer, destination buffer, image width, and height, then dispatch clEnqueueNDRangeKernel with a local work-group size of 64.
detail_opencl_pipeline_step4: Read processed pixels back to the native bitmap buffer (clEnqueueReadBuffer), release all OpenCL objects (mem, program, kernel, queue, context), unlock the Android bitmap, and return the result to Java.
detail_opencl_validation_item1: Gaussian blur (CPU sequential): 2059 ms — each pixel requires a weighted average over a 5×5 neighborhood, giving O(W×H×25) scalar operations with no parallelism.
detail_opencl_validation_item2: Gaussian blur (GPU via OpenCL): 246 ms — identical kernel logic distributed across all pixels in parallel; 8.4× speedup with no algorithmic change, demonstrating the advantage of data-parallel GPU execution.
detail_opencl_validation_item3: Grayscale (GPU): 81 ms; Rotation (GPU): 72 ms — both pixel-independent transforms run at sub-100 ms on a mobile GPU, confirming that the OpenCL pipeline overhead is amortized quickly for embarrassingly parallel workloads.
detail_opencl_debug_symptom: The application crashed immediately on launch when the camera preview feature was active on the target Android board, despite permissions being correctly declared in AndroidManifest.xml.
detail_opencl_debug_hypothesis: (1) Runtime permission request not properly handled for the target OS version; (2) Camera API incompatibility between hardware board and the Android version running on it; (3) SurfaceView lifecycle mismatch causing a null camera handle.
detail_opencl_debug_tests: Isolated the camera module into a separate test project — it functioned correctly in isolation, ruling out the permission hypothesis. Cross-referenced the board's Android version against the Camera API deprecation timeline and confirmed a known incompatibility with the specific hardware.
detail_opencl_debug_fix: Removed the camera capture flow from the main project and replaced it with BitmapFactory.decodeFile loading a pre-stored image, preserving the full OpenCL acceleration pipeline intact.
detail_opencl_debug_takeaway: Hardware-software compatibility on embedded Android requires explicit version gating; when a peripheral subsystem is unstable, decoupling it from the core compute pipeline allows the GPU acceleration work to be validated independently.
detail_opencl_skills_item1: Low-level GPGPU programming with OpenCL 1.2 C API: context management, kernel compilation, device-memory buffer I/O, and NDRange dispatch on a mobile GPU.
detail_opencl_skills_item2: Android NDK / JNI integration: authored CMakeLists.txt, linked platform-specific .so libraries, and bridged Java ↔ C across four native functions with bitmap pixel-level access.
detail_opencl_skills_item3: Performance measurement and analysis: instrumented CPU vs. GPU execution paths with System.nanoTime(), quantified 8.4× speedup, and interpreted results in terms of data-parallel workload characteristics.
detail_opencl_skills_item4: Systematic debugging on a resource-constrained embedded platform: isolated root cause of hardware API incompatibility, adapted system architecture without compromising the core deliverable.
work_can_title: CAN Bus Security Analysis and Intrusion Detection via LabVIEW
work_can_sub: Rate-based IDS with 64-bit multi-signal bit-packing in LabVIEW
detail_can_title: CAN Bus Security Analysis and Intrusion Detection via LabVIEW
detail_can_subtitle: Bit-level multi-signal packing with rate-based anomaly detection on a simulated ECU network
detail_can_overview: CAN (Controller Area Network), developed by Bosch in 1986, is the de-facto serial bus standard for inter-ECU communication in modern vehicles. However, CAN was not designed with security in mind, leaving it vulnerable to spoofing, sniffing, and message injection attacks. This project — conducted as a team of six with myself as team lead — analyzed the full CAN data frame structure and implemented a simulated CAN communication environment in LabVIEW, including a rate-based intrusion detection system. As a creative differentiator, we mapped six human biometric signals (liver function, hemoglobin, brain usage, hydration, gender, stress) into the 64-bit CAN data payload instead of conventional ECU signals, which made the bit-packing architecture both intuitive and demonstrably rigorous. The project won an Excellence Award at the final course presentation.
detail_can_point1: Led the full project as team lead — designed the 6-signal bit map independently, manually calculating bit boundaries to pack all signals within 64 bits with zero overflow.
detail_can_point2: Implemented the core LabVIEW Tx/Rx logic using bitwise AND/OR/SHIFT operations inside a Formula Node, along with a dual-version system supporting both integer (U16/U32) and floating-point (DBL) data types.
detail_can_point3: Architected the rate-based intrusion detection mechanism: a Waveform Chart monitors live packet intervals and triggers an LED alert plus automatic loop termination within a single cycle when anomalous injection is detected.
detail_can_arch_body: A standard CAN data frame consists of: SOF (1-bit dominant start), Arbitration Field (11-bit ID for priority arbitration + 1-bit RTR), Control Field (DLC: 4-bit data length code), Data Field (0–64 bits of payload), CRC Field (15-bit CRC-15 sequence for error detection), ACK Field (receiver acknowledgment), and EOF (7 recessive bits). This project used Identifier=5 and DLC=8 (full 64-bit payload). Because CAN carries no source authentication, any node on the bus can inject any message ID — the structural root cause of all three attack vectors analyzed.
detail_can_pack_body: Six biometric signals were packed into the 64-bit CAN payload without overlap: LFT (liver, bits 0–9, 10 bits), HEM (hemoglobin, bits 10–28, 19 bits), BRAIN (brain usage, bits 29–49, 21 bits), WATER (hydration, bits 50–52, 3 bits), GENDER (bit 53, 1 bit), STRESS (bits 54–63, 10 bits) — totaling exactly 64 bits. The Tx Formula Node packs these using bitwise masking and shifts (e.g., byte1 = ((LFT & 0x300) >> 8) | ((HEM & 0x3F) << 2)), while the Rx Formula Node applies the inverse to reconstruct all six values. Byte-level integrity was verified by confirming that all-max-value inputs produce byte0–byte7 = 0xFF, and mid-range BRAIN=100000 produces the expected bit patterns across byte3–byte6.
detail_can_vuln_step1: Lack of Authentication: CAN frames carry no sender credential. Any node with bus access can spoof any message ID, enabling spoofing and replay attacks. This is the foundational vulnerability of the protocol.
detail_can_vuln_step2: Broadcast Architecture: All nodes simultaneously receive every frame on the bus. There is no unicast — physical access (e.g., via OBD-II port) exposes the entire communication to passive sniffing or active frame injection.
detail_can_vuln_step3: Rate-Based IDS: Message injection attacks manifest as abnormal packet frequency. The LabVIEW Rx loop measures actual inter-packet intervals and compares them to a configured threshold. When the measured rate exceeds the threshold (e.g., 300 ms vs. 200 ms set value), a hazard LED switches from green to red and the Rx While Loop auto-terminates within one cycle — demonstrated in a live scenario during the course presentation.
detail_can_ext_heading: Extended Feature: Floating-Point & Negative Value Support
detail_can_ext_body: Beyond the baseline integer implementation, a second version extended all signal inputs to DBL (64-bit double precision). To pack floating-point values into the integer bit structure, each value is multiplied by 100 before packing and divided by 100 upon reception. Negative values (e.g., stress index = -100) are handled via offset encoding: +100 is added before packing and subtracted after unpacking, allowing the unsigned bit field to represent a signed range without additional bit overhead. This extension demonstrated practical awareness of data type constraints in real-world sensor payloads.
detail_can_skills_item1: CAN protocol internals — frame field analysis (SOF/Arbitration/DLC/CRC/ACK/EOF), multi-master arbitration, and ECU bus topology
detail_can_skills_item2: Low-level bit manipulation — bitwise AND/OR/SHIFT operations for multi-signal packing and unpacking across byte boundaries in LabVIEW Formula Node
detail_can_skills_item3: Intrusion detection systems — rate-based anomaly detection design, real-time Waveform Chart monitoring, and automatic safety shutdown logic
detail_can_skills_item4: LabVIEW (NI) — G-language dataflow programming, Front Panel UI design, While Loop / Build Array / Index Array, and integer-to-float type system extension
